exercises.title: Your first constructor
exercises.scenario: Add a basic constructor to a class.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to define a Cat class with a constructor that takes a name.
hints: Constructor name matches class name exactly
No return type (not even void)
this.name distinguishes field from parameter
Called via new ClassName(args)

exercises.title: Trace constructor execution
exercises.expected_solution:
Creating person: Aisha
Done. Name is: Aisha

test_cases.expected:
Creating person: Aisha
Done. Name is: Aisha

hints: Constructor runs FIRST when `new` is called
Then code after `new` continues
Order: constructor body → assignment → next line

exercises.title: Person with constructor
exercises.scenario: Build a class that initializes via constructor.
exercises.instructions:
Create a `Person` class with fields name (String) and age (int). Write a constructor that takes both. In main, create 2 people in one line each.

Expected output:
Aisha is 28 years old
Raj is 35 years old
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your Person class with constructor
// Then create two Person objects in main

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Aisha is 28 years old
Raj is 35 years old

hints: Constructor: public Person(String name, int age)
Use this.name = name and this.age = age
Create with: new Person("Aisha", 28)
Each object initialized in one line — way cleaner!

exercises.title: Fix the void constructor
exercises.scenario: Common mistake — adding void makes it a method, not a constructor.
exercises.instructions:
This code doesn't work as expected. The 'constructor' isn't being called. Fix it.

Expected output:
Name: Rex

bugs.issue: `public void Dog(...)` is a method, not a constructor. Constructors have NO return type. With void, this is just a method named Dog that never gets called by `new`.

hints: Constructors have NO return type, not even void
With void, this is a regular method, not a constructor
Result: new Dog(...) won't compile OR uses default constructor
Fix: remove `void` — make it `public Dog(String name)`

exercises.title: Fix the shadowed parameter
exercises.instructions:
This code COMPILES but the fields stay null/0. Fix it.

Expected output:
Name: Rex, Age: 5

bugs.issue: `name = name` assigns the parameter to itself (closer scope wins). The field stays null. Same for age.
bugs.fix: Use this.name = name and this.age = age to specify the field

hints: When parameter and field have same name, Java picks the parameter
`name = name` is parameter = parameter — does nothing useful
Use this.name to reference the field
this.name = name means 'set field name to parameter name'

exercises.title: Refactor Rectangle to use constructor
exercises.scenario: Move the manual field setup into a constructor.
exercises.instructions:
Refactor the Rectangle class to have a constructor that takes width and height. Replace the manual field setup in main with constructor call.

Expected output:
Area: 15.0
hints: Add constructor: public Rectangle(double width, double height)
Body: this.width = width; this.height = height;
In main: new Rectangle(5, 3) instead of 3 lines

exercises.title: Constructor with validation
exercises.instructions:
Create a `BankAccount` class:
- Fields: holderName (String), balance (double)
- Constructor: takes name and initial balance
- VALIDATION: throw IllegalArgumentException if balance < 0 or name is null/empty

In main:
1. Create valid account (Aisha, 1000.0) — print 'Created'
2. Try invalid (empty name, 100.0) — wrap in try/catch, print 'Rejected: <message>'

Expected output:
Created: Aisha with balance ₹1000.0
Rejected: Name cannot be empty
exercises.starter_code:
// Write BankAccount class with validating constructor
// Use try/catch in main for the invalid case

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Created: Aisha with balance ₹1000.0
Rejected: Name cannot be empty

hints: Validate BEFORE assigning fields
throw new IllegalArgumentException("message") rejects bad input
isBlank() catches empty AND whitespace-only strings
Use try/catch to handle the exception in main

exercises.title: Overloaded constructors
exercises.scenario: Provide multiple ways to create the object.
exercises.instructions:
Create a `Circle` class:
- Field: radius (double)
- Constructor 1: `Circle(double radius)` — sets the radius
- Constructor 2: `Circle()` — defaults radius to 1.0
- Method: `area()` returning π × r²

In main:
1. Create circle with radius 5 — print area
2. Create circle with no args — print area (should be π)

Expected output:
Area (r=5): 78.53981633974483
Area (default): 3.141592653589793
exercises.starter_code: // Write Circle class with two constructors
hints: Two constructors: one with parameter, one without
Same name (Circle), different parameter lists = overloading
Math.PI is the π constant
Java picks the right constructor based on arguments

exercises.title: Constructor chaining with this(...)
exercises.instructions:
Create a `Book` class with fields title, author, year (int) and pages (int).

Three constructors:
1. `Book(title, author, year, pages)` — primary, sets all fields
2. `Book(title, author, year)` — defaults pages to 0, chains to primary
3. `Book(title, author)` — defaults year=2024, chains to second constructor

In main, create 3 books — one with each constructor — and print all four fields.

Expected output:
The Hobbit by Tolkien (1937) — 310 pages
Dune by Herbert (1965) — 0 pages
Atomic Habits by Clear (2024) — 0 pages
exercises.starter_code:
// Write Book class with 3 chained constructors
// Use this(...) to chain

test_cases.expected_stdout:
The Hobbit by Tolkien (1937) — 310 pages
Dune by Herbert (1965) — 0 pages
Atomic Habits by Clear (2024) — 0 pages

hints: Primary 4-arg constructor does the actual work
3-arg constructor: this(title, author, year, 0) — defaults pages
2-arg constructor: this(title, author, 2024) — chains to 3-arg
this(...) MUST be the first statement in each constructor
Chained constructors run depth-first: 2-arg → 3-arg → 4-arg

exercises.title: Fix the this() position
exercises.instructions:
This doesn't compile. Fix the constructor chaining.

Expected output:
Dog: Rex (Labrador)

bugs.issue: `this(name, "Mixed")` must be the FIRST statement of the constructor. The println before it is illegal.
bugs.fix: Move this(...) to the top, println after

hints: Compilation error: 'call to this must be first statement in constructor'
Java enforces: this() and super() must be the FIRST line
Any other code must come AFTER the chained call
Reorder: this(...) first, println below

exercises.title: Employee with complete validation
exercises.instructions:
Create an `Employee` class with fields: id (int), name (String), salary (double), department (String).

Constructor validates:
- id > 0
- name not null/blank
- salary >= 0
- department not null/blank

In main:
1. Create valid employee — print details
2. Try id = -5 — catch and print error
3. Try salary = -1000 — catch and print error

Expected output:
Employee #101 Aisha (Engineering) - ₹85000.0
Error: ID must be positive
Error: Salary cannot be negative
exercises.starter_code: // Write Employee class with validating constructor

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Employee #101 Aisha (Engineering) - ₹85000.0
Error: ID must be positive
Error: Salary cannot be negative

hints: All 4 validations BEFORE any assignment
Order checks how you want them tried: id, name, salary, department
Each check throws IllegalArgumentException with a descriptive message
Use try/catch in main for each bad case

exercises.title: Trace constructor chaining
exercises.scenario: Multiple println statements across chained constructors.
hints: this(name, 0) calls the 2-arg constructor FIRST
2-arg runs completely: '2-arg start' → assignments → '2-arg end'
THEN control returns to 1-arg constructor, runs the println: '1-arg done'
Chained constructor runs FULLY before next code in calling constructor

exercises.title: URL parser with constructor
exercises.scenario: Parse a URL string in a constructor.
exercises.instructions:
Create a `URL` class that parses a URL string into components.

Fields: protocol (String), host (String), path (String)

Constructor takes a URL string like "https://google.com/search" and parses:
- protocol = part before "://" (e.g., "https")
- host = part after "://" and before next "/" (e.g., "google.com")
- path = part after host (e.g., "/search")

If no path, path = "/".

In main, parse 2 URLs and print components.

Expected output:
Protocol: https | Host: google.com | Path: /search
Protocol: http | Host: example.com | Path: /
exercises.starter_code:
// Write URL class with parsing constructor
// Parse: protocol://host/path

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Protocol: https | Host: google.com | Path: /search
Protocol: http | Host: example.com | Path: /

hints: Use indexOf("://") to find protocol boundary
substring(0, x) for protocol (before x)
substring(x + 3) for everything after ://
Check if rest contains "/" (pathStart)
If yes: split host and path. If no: whole rest is host, path = '/'

exercises.title: Point class with multiple constructors
exercises.instructions:
Create a `Point` class for 2D coordinates:
- Fields: x (double), y (double)
- Constructor 1: `Point(x, y)` — primary
- Constructor 2: `Point()` — origin (0, 0). Chains to primary.
- Constructor 3: `Point(Point other)` — COPY constructor. Chains to primary with other.x, other.y.
- Method: `distanceFrom(Point other)` — returns Euclidean distance

In main, demonstrate all 3 constructors and the distance method.

Expected output:
P1: (3.0, 4.0)
P2 (origin): (0.0, 0.0)
P3 (copy of P1): (3.0, 4.0)
Distance from P1 to origin: 5.0
exercises.starter_code:
// Write Point class with 3 constructors and distanceFrom method
// Use Math.sqrt and Math.pow for distance

test_cases.expected_stdout:
P1: (3.0, 4.0)
P2 (origin): (0.0, 0.0)
P3 (copy of P1): (3.0, 4.0)
Distance from P1 to origin: 5.0

hints: Primary constructor: this.x = x; this.y = y;
No-arg: this(0, 0) — chains to primary with origin
Copy constructor: this(other.x, other.y) — accesses other's fields
distanceFrom: √((x1-x2)² + (y1-y2)²)
Pythagorean: 3-4-5 triangle → distance from (3,4) to (0,0) is 5

exercises.title: Order with line items
exercises.scenario: Multi-class system with constructor validation throughout.
exercises.instructions:
Build TWO classes:

1. `OrderItem`:
   - Fields: productName (String), quantity (int), unitPrice (double)
   - Constructor validates: name non-empty, quantity > 0, unitPrice >= 0
   - Method `lineTotal()`: quantity × unitPrice

2. `Order`:
   - Fields: orderId (int), customerName (String), items (OrderItem[])
   - Constructor: takes id, name, items[]. Validates: id > 0, name non-empty, items not null/empty
   - Method `total()`: sums lineTotal() of all items

In main:
1. Create 3 OrderItems
2. Create Order #1001 for Aisha with those items
3. Print order total
4. Try invalid order (empty items array) — catch and print error

Expected output:
Order #1001 (Aisha) total: ₹2447.0
Error: Order must have at least one item
exercises.starter_code:
// Build OrderItem and Order classes
// Both classes have validating constructors

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Order #1001 (Aisha) total: ₹2447.0
Error: Order must have at least one item

hints: Two classes — both have validating constructors
OrderItem.lineTotal = quantity × unitPrice
Order.total loops through items and sums lineTotal()
Order validation: items != null AND items.length > 0
This is REAL e-commerce backend pattern
